serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of crab and tomato vermicelli soup, approximately 450 g, containing a generous portion of crab meat, prawns, rice vermicelli noodles, tomato broth, and assorted vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Crab, cooked; Rice vermicelli, cooked; Tomato, raw — composite estimation from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and HPB Singapore Foodo reference values for seafood noodle soups
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service (FoodData Central SR Legacy) and Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this exact composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from component-level data: crab meat (USDA FDC SR Legacy #15139, blue crab cooked), rice vermicelli cooked (USDA FDC SR Legacy #20065 adapted), tomato broth and vegetables (USDA FDC SR Legacy #11529 tomato raw). HPB Singapore Foodo seafood bee hoon soup entries were used as a cross-reference anchor for the composite dish profile. Estimated dish composition: approximately 30% crab/prawn, 25% rice vermicelli, 35% broth, 10% vegetables. Per-100g values reflect the mixed bowl composition.

gi_source.title: Rice vermicelli, cooked — GI value from University of Sydney GI Database; composite dish GI estimated by weighted carbohydrate contribution
gi_source.source_match_notes: Rice vermicelli (bee hoon) has a published GI of approximately 58 in the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). The composite dish GI is estimated at 53 after accounting for the diluting effect of high-protein crab and prawn content, tomato broth, and vegetables, which lower the overall glycaemic response relative to plain vermicelli. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (53 × 36.9) / 100 ≈ 19.6. Serving carbohydrate: 8.2 g/100g × 450 g serving = 36.9 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from seafood broth
hypertension.guidance: At approximately 420 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 1890 mg per bowl), this dish contributes a substantial portion of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced-sodium broth or limit portion size to half a bowl.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice vermicelli carbohydrates
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic index of 53 (low) but a glycemic load of approximately 19.6 per bowl, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size of the vermicelli component and pair the dish with additional non-starchy vegetables to moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and dietary cholesterol from crab and prawns
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish provides lean seafood protein with low saturated fat (0.2 g/100 g), which is broadly cardioprotective, but the high sodium content and moderate dietary cholesterol from crustaceans (approximately 42 mg/100 g) warrant caution for individuals with established cardiovascular disease; choosing a smaller portion and requesting less salt in the broth is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crab and Tomato Vermicelli Soup is a Singaporean seafood noodle soup dish featuring fresh crab meat and prawns simmered in a tangy, lightly sweetened tomato-based broth, served over soft rice vermicelli noodles (bee hoon) with assorted vegetables such as bean sprouts, leafy greens, and tomato wedges. The dish is a staple of Singapore's Vietnamese-influenced seafood noodle restaurant scene, popularised by establishments such as Viet'Spice. The broth is characteristically bright red-orange from ripe tomatoes, with a mildly acidic and umami-rich flavour profile derived from the natural sweetness of fresh crab and the acidity of tomatoes. The texture contrasts soft, slippery vermicelli noodles with tender crab meat and firm prawn flesh. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-calorie, high-protein meal (approximately 62 kcal and 5.8 g protein per 100 g) with low saturated fat, making it a relatively lean seafood option. However, the sodium content is high due to the broth, which is a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 53 (low GI), though the glycemic load per bowl is moderate at approximately 19.6, relevant for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is rich in vitamin B12, zinc, and selenium from the crustacean components. It is typically served as a main meal and is priced at the premium end of hawker-style dishes in Singapore.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this exact composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data for crab, rice vermicelli, and tomato, cross-referenced against HPB Singapore Foodo seafood bee hoon soup entries. GI is an analogous estimate based on published rice vermicelli GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database, adjusted for the protein and vegetable dilution effect. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level sources are high quality and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component entries (crab cooked #15139, rice vermicelli #20065, tomato raw #11529) and cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo seafood bee hoon soup reference profiles. No single approved-source entry exists for this exact dish. 2. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources used for this estimation. 3. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources used for this estimation. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed zero as this is a minimally processed seafood and vegetable soup with no hydrogenated fat ingredients. 5. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate based on rice vermicelli GI (approximately 58, University of Sydney GI Database) adjusted downward for the high-protein crustacean content and vegetable dilution effect in the composite dish. 6. Halal status is marked false as this cannot be confirmed without restaurant-level certification; the dish contains crustaceans which are permissible under most halal standards but certification is unverified. 7. The restaurant source (Viet'Spice, Circular Road, Singapore) is noted for provenance context only and is not used as a nutritional evidence source. 8. Price data (SGD 21.90) is preserved from the input record for reference.
